critique.summary: The article explains strategies for deferring U.S. taxes by reinvesting taxable income into business expenses and leveraging depreciation schedules. It emphasizes that these methods are legal and intended by the tax system to encourage economic growth, while also discussing loan refinancing and inheritance tax implications.
mainPoints: Taxes can be deferred by reinvesting income into business expenses and depreciable assets.
Depreciation schedules can be adjusted to minimize taxable income in profitable years.
Refinancing loans can provide cash without triggering taxable income.
The government incentivizes economic growth through tax policies that reward reinvestment.
Inheritance can reset cost bases, effectively eliminating deferred tax obligations upon death.
Some tax incentives may be exploited as loopholes, but distinguishing legitimate use from abuse is difficult.
critique.truthfulness: The article accurately describes common and legal tax deferral strategies such as depreciation, reinvestment of business expenses, and loan refinancing, which are well-established practices in U.S. tax law. It correctly notes that loans are not taxable income and that inheritance can reset asset cost bases, reducing tax liabilities. However, the article simplifies complex tax rules and omits important legal nuances and risks involved in these strategies. The mention of Modern Monetary Theory is tangential and not directly relevant to tax deferral legality. Overall, the claims are mostly truthful but presented with some informal language and lacking detailed citations.
considerations: The article does not discuss the legal risks and IRS scrutiny associated with aggressive depreciation or loan refinancing strategies.
It omits mention of the Alternative Minimum Tax (AMT) and other tax provisions that can limit deferral benefits.
There is no discussion of the potential consequences of tax evasion versus legal tax avoidance.
The article does not explain the complexity and costs of cost segregation studies or refinancing processes.
It does not address state and local tax variations or international tax implications.
The ethical considerations of tax deferral and avoidance strategies are only briefly mentioned.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around strategies for deferring US taxes, particularly through reinvestment and leveraging assets, with some commenters highlighting risks such as IRS audits and the ethical implications of tax avoidance. There is also comparison to Canadian tax treatment at death and skepticism about the fairness and practicality of tax deferral methods.

comments.author: nayuki
comments.text:
> Loaned money isn't taxable income, so you can save/spend it without affecting your tax rate.

> Death is a popular escape from deferred taxes. When you die, your obligations to the government vanish. Your heirs inherit assets/property at market value. Their assets depreciate from new cost bases.

The article talks about taxes in the USA, and I think the treatment of taxes at death is unfair by giving a significant tax advantage to people who hold assets till death, especially with the step-up basis. The way Canada handles it seems more reasonable to me:

> Capital property generally includes real estate, such as homes and cottages, investments like stocks, mutual funds or crypto-assets, and personal belongings like artwork, collections or jewelry. When a person dies, they are considered to have sold all their property just prior to death, even though there is no actual disposition or sale. This is called a deemed disposition and may result in a capital gain or capital loss

-- https://www.canada.ca/en/revenue-agency/services/tax/individ...

In exchange, Canada does not have an inheritance tax. All taxation is resolved in the estate of the deceased person before the money or assets are passed on without further taxation.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a well-informed comparison between US and Canadian tax systems regarding inheritance and capital gains, highlighting a legitimate policy difference and its implications.

comments.author: jeffreyrogers
comments.text:
Pretty good overview of how/why these deductions reduce your taxable income. Couple of things to note.

Depreciation is recaptured if you sell an asset for more than its depreciated basis. People sometimes get into trouble with this if they rapidly depreciate real estate and then sell it. Even if you sell for less than your purchase price it is possible to owe taxes.

You also aren't going to be able to pay no taxes since you do need to realize some income to pay for mortgage/rent, food, transportation, etc. I guess if you had assets you could borrow against it would be possible to pay for these using the loan proceeds (which are not taxable).
comments.analysis: The comment accurately explains depreciation recapture and the tax risks involved in aggressive depreciation strategies, adding practical caution to the discussion.

comments.author: kg
comments.text:
> Defer US taxes by reinvesting your taxable income into the economy as business expenses, depreciating assets, etc.

Be really careful when doing this. Make sure you have a great accountant - if you go more than a few years without turning a measurable profit, your risk of being audited apparently goes up. My accountant personally cautioned me about this since my business has been in an R&D phase for 5 years so we've been showing a small loss every year. The last thing you want is for the IRS to decide you've been cheating on your taxes.
comments.analysis: This comment offers practical advice about the importance of professional tax help and the audit risks associated with prolonged losses, which is a credible concern.

comments.author: PopAlongKid
comments.text:
>Death is a popular escape from deferred taxes. When you die, your obligations to the government vanish. Your heirs inherit assets/property at market value. Their assets depreciate from new cost bases.

The article only addresses a subset of economic activity. The larger portion of the adult population are wage earners or retirees, not business owners. For them, large investments in Traditional IRAs or 401k plans are most definitely not able to escape upon death the income taxes that were deferred.
comments.analysis: This comment correctly points out that tax deferral strategies discussed mainly benefit business owners and investors, not the majority of wage earners or retirees.

comments.author: yonixw
comments.text:
> For your leveraged investments, pay yourself in refinanced cash when your investments appreciate and/or credit rates drop.

In other words: Gamble that (1) your investments appreciate, or (2) that you will find credit rates drop when convenient.

In 1 word: Gamble.

So, either you are rich and have spare money to gamble, which sure, might be beneficial against taxes. But you could also gamble against any other sector (stocks, housing, startups...)

Or, if you are not rich, just put it in the 401k (or eq).
comments.analysis: The comment insightfully critiques the risk involved in leveraging investments for tax deferral, framing it as a gamble which is a valid perspective.

comments.author: fogzen
comments.text: You can’t legally reclassify all your expenses as reinvestment. The IRS will determine what is actually an expenditure, and there are rules around it.
comments.analysis: This comment correctly warns about IRS rules limiting expense classification, providing a necessary legal caution to tax deferral strategies.

comments.author: josefritzishere
comments.text: This feels like a great way to get audited by the IRS. It does not feel like sound advice.
comments.analysis: This comment expresses a reasonable concern about the risk of IRS audits when aggressively deferring taxes, reflecting common taxpayer fears.

comments.author: buellerbueller
comments.text: Or, just pay your taxes.  We collectively benefit from them.
comments.analysis: This comment appeals to civic responsibility and the social benefits of paying taxes, though it lacks detailed analysis.

comments.author: SoftTalker
comments.text:
It seems to me that I'm running into more people who just don't file their taxes. They wait for the IRS to send them a letter saying how much they owe, and they just pay that.

I can't figure out the thought process of someone who finds this sensible. Maybe there isn't one.
comments.analysis: This comment highlights a behavioral trend that may be true anecdotally but lacks supporting data or deeper insight.

comments.author: deadbabe
comments.text: We should force cost basis to rise some % every few years, in order make tax due on unrealized gains. How would that throw a wrench into these tax deferral schemes?
comments.analysis: This comment proposes a policy idea to tax unrealized gains by adjusting cost basis, which is a recognized concept in tax reform discussions.
